one_line_takeaway: A static, GitHub-enriched snapshot of ~14K MCP servers/clients normalized to a 26-field schema, useful as an ecosystem-measurement and code-linking substrate for later MCP security auditing — but the paper itself delivers a dataset, not security findings.
key_finding: The MCP ecosystem shows a highly skewed long-tail popularity distribution and heavy concentration in a few languages. For servers, 10,489 of ~13.9K have 0-10 GitHub stars while only 94 exceed 5K stars; server languages are dominated by Python (4,546), TypeScript (3,787), and JavaScript (2,083). The language concentration persists even when filtered to top-starred projects, indicating these languages dominate both the long tail and the well-maintained core.
core_contribution: MCPCorpus, a large-scale, continuously-updatable dataset of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) ecosystem, together with utility tooling and a web search interface. The authors curate ~14K MCP artifacts (13,875 servers and 300 clients) by crawling the MCP.so registry and enriching each entry with GitHub repository metadata, normalizing everything into a unified schema of 20+ (up to 26) attributes covering identity, interface/configuration, GitHub activity signals, and code metadata. The paper's own contribution is the resource (dataset + sync/normalization/inspection tools + web UI), not a new security method or empirical security study; security auditing is presented only as a proposed downstream application enabled by the interface- and code-metadata fields.
threat_model: The paper does not define or evaluate a threat model of its own. It is a resource/dataset paper. It gestures at motivating security concerns cited from other work (malicious server behaviors, inconsistent implementations, weak protocol compliance, e.g. the WhatsApp MCP exfiltration blog and third-party MCP attack-vector papers), and proposes that fields like server_command, server_config, tools, and sse_url expose an 'external interface surface' that future work could use to assess attack exposure or command-execution behavior. But no attacker model, no vulnerability scan, and no security findings are produced or measured in this paper.
limitations: (1) Static-only: attributes come from registry text and GitHub metadata, with no runtime or live-service interaction, so no behavioral/dynamic security signal is captured. (2) No security analysis is actually performed — security auditing is only a proposed application, so the paper cannot substantiate its security-utility claims. (3) Registry coverage rests primarily on a single hub (MCP.so) plus GitHub; other package managers/hubs mentioned as motivation are not integrated. (4) Classification is currently limited to three dimensions (type, category, language); interface type, officiality, and maintenance status are described as future work. (5) Snapshot dependence: counts are tied to a 3 June 2025 crawl of a database that updates daily, so figures are point-in-time. (6) The PDF carries placeholder venue metadata ("Conference'17", 2017 dates), consistent with an unreviewed arXiv preprint. (7) Deduplication heuristics (highest-activity or earliest-created) may drop legitimate divergent forks.

datasets_used: MCPCorpus (the artifact this paper introduces): 13,875 MCP servers + 300 MCP clients (~14K artifacts), each with up to 26 structured attributes, stored as JSONL
MCP.so registry (primary source; as of 3 June 2025 hosting >14,000 servers and 300 clients; ~13.9K servers and 300 clients extracted at collection time)
GitHub REST API (enrichment source for stars, forks, issues, contributors, last commit, license, language composition, file presence)
quantitative_results: Dataset size: 13,875 servers and 300 clients; up to 26 schema fields per record (20+ normalized attributes)
Server primary-language counts: Python=4,546; TypeScript=3,787; JavaScript=2,083; Go=589; Java=359; Rust=152; C#=132; HTML=98
Client primary-language counts: Python=133; TypeScript=86; JavaScript=14; Go=8; Vue=7; Rust=5; Dart=4; Java=3
Example artifact edgeone-pages-mcp (TencentEdgeOne): 125 stars, 16 forks, MIT license, last commit May 2025
Example artifact playwright-mcp (Microsoft): 11,162 stars, 727 forks, 22 open issues, 24 contributors, Apache License 2.0, last commit 2025-06-03
